content.text: The Verification Process Overview

Account verification in AWS is a two-part process: payment method validation (to confirm a real payment instrument exists before any charges occur) and identity verification (to confirm there is a real person behind the account). AWS uses these checks to reduce fraud and abuse, and both must pass before the account becomes fully active.

Neither step results in an immediate charge to your card. The payment step places a temporary authorization hold; identity verification uses a phone call or SMS. Together they take about 3 minutes if you have your card and phone ready.
Verification Flow: Four Stages

attrs.alt: AWS account verification flow: payment method entry, $1 authorization hold, phone identity verification, account activation — with accepted payment types and billing cycle details

content.text: The flow above shows how each stage feeds the next. The card authorization proves the payment instrument is real and active. Phone verification proves a human is behind the account. Only after both pass does AWS begin the activation review that grants full account access.
Step 1: Adding a Payment Method

During sign-up, you are prompted to enter credit or debit card details. AWS accepts Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and Discover. The card number, expiration date, and CVV are collected and tokenized — AWS does not store raw card numbers.

The billing address entered here is used for tax calculations and invoice formatting. It should match the address on file with your card issuer. A mismatch can cause the authorization step to fail even if the card number is correct.
What AWS Does NOT Accept
Prepaid debit cards and gift cards — these are often rejected because they cannot be billed reliably for ongoing charges
Virtual card numbers (single-use or merchant-specific numbers) — they expire after one use and cannot cover a month-end invoice
Digital wallets (PayPal, Apple Pay, Google Pay) — not supported at account creation; only credit/debit cards at signup
Cryptocurrency — AWS invoices in fiat currency only
Bank transfers or checks at sign-up — some enterprise arrangements allow invoicing later, but the initial account creation requires a card

If you need to use a virtual card number for security reasons, use one that has a high enough limit and a long enough validity period to cover ongoing monthly bills — AWS will bill it monthly for the account's lifetime. A card that expires in 30 days will cause payment failures.
Step 2: The $1 Authorization Hold

Immediately after you submit your card details, AWS places a $1.00 USD authorization hold (also called a pre-authorization or pending charge). This is a standard card network transaction that asks your bank: 'Is this card real, active, and able to accept charges?' — without actually moving money.
What to Expect
Aspect
Detail
Amount
$1.00 USD (or equivalent in local currency for non-US cards)
Appears as
A pending transaction on your statement, often labeled 'Amazon Web Services' or 'AWS'
Duration
Reversed automatically within 3–5 business days; some banks take up to 10 days
If it fails
AWS shows an error; try a different card or contact your bank to allow international/online charges
Real charge?
No — it is an authorization only; the funds are never actually transferred to AWS

Some banks flag the $1 AWS authorization as suspicious, especially for first-time transactions. If the authorization fails, check whether your bank has international online transaction restrictions enabled. A quick call to your bank to authorize the transaction usually resolves it.
Verifying the Hold Cleared

# There is no AWS CLI command to check payment status during sign-up.
# After account activation, check billing status via:
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=2024-01-01,End=2024-02-01 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics BlendedCost \
  --query 'ResultsByTime[].Total.BlendedCost'

# Check account payment status
aws account get-contact-information
# (Only after activation; returns account billing contact info)
Step 3: Phone Identity Verification

After the payment authorization succeeds, AWS asks you to verify a phone number. This step confirms there is a real person behind the account — not an automated script creating accounts in bulk. AWS uses a 4-digit PIN delivered via SMS or automated voice call.
How the Phone Verification Works
Enter your phone number in international format (e.g., +1 for US, +44 for UK)
Choose SMS (text message) or Voice call
AWS calls or texts within seconds with a 4-digit PIN
Enter the PIN in the verification field on the web page
If the PIN fails, you can retry — but after 2-3 failures, AWS enforces a wait period

The phone number you verify here is not permanently attached to the account — it is only used for this verification step. The account's contact phone number (which is used for support and billing alerts) is set separately in Account Settings.

Use a phone number that accepts international calls. If you are signing up from a country where AWS might route from a US number, a phone that blocks international SMS could prevent the verification code from arriving. VOIP numbers (Google Voice, Skype, etc.) sometimes work but are less reliable.
If Verification Fails
Failure Reason
Solution
No SMS received
Wait 60 seconds and try the 'Resend code' option; switch to voice call if SMS fails
Code expired
Codes are typically valid for 10 minutes; request a new one
Wrong number format
Enter the full international format including country code (+1 for US)
VOIP number rejected
Use a real mobile or landline number
Too many attempts
Wait the displayed time (usually 30 minutes) then retry
International restrictions
Contact AWS Support if your country's numbers are not accepted
Step 4: Account Activation Timeline

After phone verification, you choose a support plan (Basic is free and is the default). AWS then reviews the information you provided and activates the account. This process is usually instant or takes a few minutes, but AWS's terms allow up to 24 hours.
Service
Availability After Activation
S3 (object storage)
Usually available within 1-2 minutes of activation
IAM (user management)
Available immediately after activation
EC2 (virtual machines)
May take 5-15 minutes; some instance types need account warm-up
Marketplace AMIs
Available once EC2 is active
Support cases
Available immediately after plan selection
Cost Explorer
Data begins appearing 24 hours after first usage
Organizations
Available immediately; member accounts take 15 minutes to join

The activation email arrives at your root user email address with the subject 'Your AWS Account is Ready'. If you do not receive it within 24 hours, check your spam folder. The account may already be active even before the email arrives — try signing in to the console to check.
Billing Currency and Payment Processing
By default, AWS bills in USD. For accounts in certain regions, AWS may bill in local currency:
India: billed in INR (Indian Rupee) through AWS India subsidiary
Japan: billed in JPY through Amazon Japan
Brazil: billed in BRL in some account configurations
Most other regions: billed in USD regardless of where the account is located

For USD-billed accounts outside the US, your credit card issuer applies currency conversion and may charge a foreign transaction fee (typically 1-3%). This is charged by your bank, not AWS, and is not visible on the AWS invoice.
How AWS Charges Work Monthly

# View your current month's estimated charges:
aws ce get-cost-forecast \
  --time-period Start=2024-06-01,End=2024-07-01 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metric BLENDED_COST

# View actual charges for last month:
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=2024-05-01,End=2024-06-01 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics AmortizedCost UnblendedCost \
  --query 'ResultsByTime[].Total'

AWS usage charges accrue throughout the calendar month and are invoiced on the 1st through 3rd of the following month. The invoice is generated first, then payment is charged automatically to your default payment method. If you are within the Free Tier, applicable charges are subtracted before billing.
Updating Payment Methods
After account creation, you can add, update, or change the default payment method at any time through the Billing Console:
Log in as the root user (updating payment methods is root-only)
Navigate to Billing and Cost Management -> Payment methods
Click Add payment method to add a new card
Click Make default next to the card you want to use for future invoices
Remove old cards after confirming the new one is set as default

# Payment method management requires console access.
# You can verify billing contact info via CLI:
aws account get-contact-information \
  --query 'ContactInformation.{Name:FullName, Address:AddressLine1, City:City}'

# Check for pending payment failures:
aws health describe-events \
  --filter '{"eventTypeCategories":["accountNotification"]}' \
  --query 'events[?contains(eventTypeCode, `BILLING`)].{Code:eventTypeCode, Status:statusCode}'
Payment Failure Handling
If a payment fails (expired card, insufficient funds, card blocked), AWS follows a grace period process before suspending the account:
Day After Invoice
AWS Action
Invoice date
First charge attempt — immediate payment or failure notification
~4 days
Second payment attempt if first failed
~7 days
Final payment attempt; email warning sent to root email
~14 days
Account suspended — new resources cannot be created
~60 days
Account flagged for termination; resources scheduled for deletion
~90 days
Resources permanently deleted; account closed

AWS does not publicly document exact grace period timelines as they can vary. The important point is that a payment failure does not immediately kill your account — you have time to update your payment method and clear the balance. Log in to the Billing Console and go to Payment methods -> Make payment to pay any outstanding balance manually.

Set up a billing alert (CloudWatch alarm on EstimatedCharges) so you are never surprised by an invoice amount. An unexpected large bill is much easier to manage before the payment date than after a failed charge.
Tax Information and Invoices
AWS applies taxes based on the registered address of the account. The exact tax treatment depends on your location and account type (personal vs. business):
United States: AWS collects sales tax in states where it is required by law. Tax-exempt organizations can upload exemption certificates in Billing -> Tax settings.
European Union: VAT applies. Enter your VAT registration number in Tax settings to receive invoices with the correct VAT treatment (business-to-business reverse charge in many cases).
United Kingdom: VAT applies post-Brexit through AWS EMEA SARL. Enter your UK VAT registration number.
India: GST applies. Enter your GSTIN in Tax settings. AWS bills through Amazon Web Services India Private Limited for accounts registered in India.
Other regions: Check the AWS Tax Help documentation for country-specific requirements.

# Retrieve your current invoices (requires root or billing access):
aws invoicing list-invoices \
  --query 'Invoices[].{ID:InvoiceId, Date:IssuedAt, Amount:TotalAmount}' \
  --output table

# Download a specific invoice PDF (via Billing console - no CLI equivalent)
# Billing -> Bills -> select month -> Download PDF/CSV
Understanding Account Types: Individual vs. Professional

During the contact information phase of sign-up, AWS asks you to choose an account type: Personal or Professional (sometimes labeled Individual or Company). This choice affects how AWS formats invoices, what tax information is requested, and how AWS Support interacts with you — but it does not restrict what services you can use.
Aspect
Personal / Individual
Professional / Company
Who it is for
Individuals, hobbyists, personal projects
Businesses, startups, enterprises
Invoice format
Shows individual name and address
Shows company name, VAT number, business address
Tax treatment
Personal tax rules apply
Business tax rules; VAT reverse charge available in EU
Support channel
Standard Support terms
Same terms; business contact for enterprise agreements
AWS Marketplace
Personal subscriptions
Company-owned subscriptions; License Manager integration
Account ownership
Tied to individual identity
Tied to company; easier to transfer ownership

You can change your account type after creation in Account -> Account Settings -> Account type. The change affects future invoices and tax calculations but does not alter any existing resources or access configuration.

For any account that will be used for business purposes — even a side project that might one day generate revenue — choose Professional. It makes tax compliance simpler and keeps billing records in the company name from day one.
How the $1 Authorization Interacts With Your Bank
The exact experience of the $1 authorization varies by bank, card type, and country. Understanding what your bank sees helps you anticipate and resolve issues:
What Your Bank Records

Your bank sees a standard authorization request from 'Amazon Web Services' or 'AWS' for $1.00. The bank records it as a pending transaction, which places a hold on $1 of your available credit or funds. The merchant category code (MCC) AWS uses is typically 7372 (Computer Programming, Data Processing) or 7374 (Computer Maintenance and Repair, Data Processing Services).

The hold reduces your available balance by $1 until it is released. AWS sends a void (cancellation) request to your bank after the authorization is confirmed, which triggers the release. However, some banks process voids slowly — this is a bank-side behavior, not an AWS behavior.
Scenarios Where the Authorization Fails
Daily online transaction limit reached: some cards have daily limits on online purchases; the $1 authorization counts toward this limit
International transaction blocking: if your card has restrictions on transactions from foreign merchants, the AWS authorization (which may route through US or Irish billing entities) may be blocked
Velocity checks: if you have recently had many card authorization attempts (e.g., from failed sign-up retries), your bank's fraud detection may temporarily block new authorizations
Insufficient funds: for debit cards, the card account balance must have at least $1 available
Card restricted to specific merchant categories: some corporate cards are locked to specific MCC codes and reject general-purpose online charges

If the authorization fails, AWS displays an error message immediately. The most effective resolution is to call the number on the back of your card and ask the bank to allow a one-time charge from 'Amazon Web Services' or to temporarily lift online purchase restrictions.
Free Tier and Its Relationship to Billing

New AWS accounts receive 12 months of Free Tier access for many services, plus some services that are always free. Understanding how Free Tier interacts with payment is important: even though many services are free, your payment method must still be on file and valid.
Free Tier Type
Duration
Examples
What Happens After
12-month free
First 12 months from account creation date
EC2 t2/t3.micro (750h/mo), S3 (5 GB), RDS (750h/mo)
Standard pricing applies automatically — no notification
Always free
Indefinitely
Lambda (1M requests/mo), DynamoDB (25 GB), CloudWatch (10 metrics)
Continues indefinitely within limits
Free trials
Varies by service (30-60 days)
SageMaker (2 months), Comprehend, Lex
Billing starts after trial expires

The Free Tier is applied automatically — you do not need to enable it or sign up separately. AWS tracks your Free Tier usage in Billing -> Free Tier. When you approach or exceed a Free Tier limit, AWS does NOT stop the service; it begins charging standard rates. This is the most common source of unexpected first bills for new accounts.

# Check your current Free Tier usage
aws ce get-dimension-values \
  --time-period Start=2024-06-01,End=2024-07-01 \
  --dimension SERVICE \
  --query 'DimensionValues[].Value'

# Set up a billing alarm for the Free Tier (via CloudWatch)
# First, switch to us-east-1 — billing metrics only exist there
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name free-tier-exceed-warning \
  --metric-name EstimatedCharges \
  --namespace AWS/Billing \
  --statistic Maximum \
  --period 86400 \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --threshold 1.00 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:billing-alerts \
  --region us-east-1
# Replace the SNS ARN with one you create for your account

Set a billing alarm for $1 immediately after account creation. This gives you early warning the moment any charge appears, even if you believe everything is within the Free Tier. Misconfiguring a single service (like leaving an NAT Gateway running) can generate significant charges outside the Free Tier.
Consolidated Billing in AWS Organizations

If your account is part of or will join an AWS Organization, payment flows through the organization's management account rather than each member account's individual payment method. Understanding how this works prevents confusion:
The management account's payment method is charged for all usage across all member accounts in the organization
Member accounts do not need their own payment methods after joining an organization (the management account covers billing)
Each member account still receives its own invoice showing its portion of usage
Volume discounts aggregate across all accounts in the organization, often reducing per-unit pricing
Free Tier allowances are per-account, not shared — each member account gets its own 12-month free tier

When creating a fresh account that will immediately join an Organization, the verification flow still requires a payment method at sign-up. After joining the Organization, the member account's payment method is superseded by the management account's — but the initial sign-up card information must still be valid to pass verification.

# After joining an organization, check which account pays the bill:
aws organizations describe-account \
  --account-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text) \
  --query 'Account.{Id:Id, JoinedMethod:JoinedMethod, Status:Status}'

# View consolidated billing status
aws organizations describe-organization \
  --query 'Organization.{Id:Id, MasterAccountId:MasterAccountId, FeatureSet:FeatureSet}'
# FeatureSet: "ALL" means billing consolidation + SCPs are active
Invoice Structure and Understanding Your First Bill

Your first AWS invoice (if you have any charges after the Free Tier) will appear in the Billing Console around the 3rd of the month following your first chargeable usage. The invoice structure breaks down costs by:
Service: the AWS service that generated the charge (e.g., Amazon EC2, Amazon S3, AWS Data Transfer)
Region: the AWS region where the resources were running (some services like data transfer are billed globally)
Usage type: the specific resource type within the service (e.g., BoxUsage:t3.micro for EC2, or TimedStorage-ByteHrs for S3)
Quantity: the amount of usage in the service's unit (hours, GB-hours, API calls, etc.)
Rate: the per-unit price applied (may be tiered with volume discounts)
Amount: the total charge for that line item
Download the invoice as a CSV to analyze charges in a spreadsheet. The CSV provides more detail than the PDF invoice, including individual resource IDs when cost allocation tags are enabled.

# List available invoices (requires billing permissions)
aws invoicing list-invoices \
  --query 'Invoices[].{InvoiceId:InvoiceId, Month:IssuedAt, Amount:TotalAmount}' \
  --output table

# Analyze costs by service for the previous month
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=2024-05-01,End=2024-06-01 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics UnblendedCost \
  --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE \
  --query 'ResultsByTime[0].Groups[].{Service:Keys[0], Cost:Metrics.UnblendedCost.Amount}' \
  --output table
Common Verification Problems and Resolutions
The following issues occur frequently during account verification. This reference saves time if you encounter them:
Problem
Likely Cause
Resolution
'Payment information appears to be invalid'
Card declined at authorization
Try a different card; call your bank to allow AWS charges
'Identity verification failed'
Phone number not receiving SMS
Switch to voice call; use a mobile number not VOIP
Account stuck in 'Pending' for hours
Manual review triggered (common for new card types or unusual sign-up patterns)
Wait up to 24h; contact AWS Support if no activation email after 24h
$1 charge appears multiple times
Multiple sign-up attempts with the same card
All will reverse within 5 business days; only one account is created
'We could not verify your identity'
Phone carrier issues or number format wrong
Include country code; try a different phone number
Activation email never arrived
Email in spam or delayed
Check spam; try signing in directly at console.aws.amazon.com
Post-Verification: Confirming Your Account Is Fully Active
Once you receive the activation email, run a quick set of checks to confirm all services are accessible and billing is correctly configured before doing any real work:

#!/bin/bash
# Quick post-activation health check

echo "=== Account Identity ==="
aws sts get-caller-identity

echo ""
echo "=== Billing Alert Setup ==="
# Ensure billing alerts are enabled (required to use CloudWatch billing metrics)
aws account get-contact-information --query 'ContactInformation.FullName'

echo ""
echo "=== Service Access Check ==="
# Verify S3 is accessible (usually the first service available)
aws s3 ls 2>&1 | head -5
# An empty output means S3 is active but no buckets exist — that is expected

echo ""
echo "=== IAM Summary ==="
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.{
  Users: Users,
  Roles: Roles,
  MFAEnabled: AccountMFAEnabled,
  AccessKeys: AccountAccessKeysPresent
}'

If the S3 check returns a 'Could not connect to the endpoint URL' error, the account may still be activating. Wait a few minutes and retry. EC2 and other compute services may take an additional 5-15 minutes to become fully accessible.
Credit and Promotional Credits

AWS occasionally issues promotional credits to accounts — for example, through startup programs (AWS Activate), academic programs, or re:Invent giveaways. Credits appear as line items in the Billing Console and are applied to invoices before the payment method is charged.
Credits are applied automatically to eligible charges at invoice time; you do not need to enter a code at payment
Credits have expiration dates — unused credits expire at the end of the stated period
Credits do not cover all services; the credit description specifies which services are eligible
Multiple credits stack — all valid, eligible credits are applied before the card is charged
AWS Activate credits (for startups) require applying through the Activate portal and are credited after approval

# View available credits and their balances
aws ce list-cost-allocation-tags --query 'CostAllocationTags[?Status==`Active`]'

# Credits appear in the billing console -> Credits section
# No direct CLI to list credits; use the console or billing API:
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=2024-06-01,End=2024-07-01 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics UnblendedCost \
  --filter '{"Dimensions":{"Key":"RECORD_TYPE","Values":["Credit"]}}' \
  --query 'ResultsByTime[0].Total'

If you are creating an account for a startup, apply for AWS Activate through the Activate portal before your first month of usage. Activate credits can be substantial (up to $100,000 for portfolio companies) and are forfeited if you do not apply within the eligibility window.
Summary

AWS account verification involves two distinct checks: payment method validation (which places a temporary $1 authorization hold that reverses within days) and phone identity verification (a 4-digit PIN by SMS or voice call). Neither results in an immediate real charge. The account activates within minutes to 24 hours depending on AWS processing time.

Accepted payment methods are standard credit and debit cards — prepaid cards, virtual card numbers, and digital wallets are generally rejected. AWS bills monthly in USD (or local currency in select regions), with invoices generated in the first days of each month. Tax treatment depends on your location and account type; enter tax registration numbers in Billing settings to ensure correct invoicing from the start.
